# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Sunday, March 1, 2026**

Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.

The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.

Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dy
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
een Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).

As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Cate
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
ic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.

For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Oran
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.

Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.

After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerge
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
dating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.

According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
m (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.

According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.

Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin
label: from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory/]
to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.

The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

# Artistry and Unity Take Center Stage at PASE Community Concert 2026

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**

The PASE Community Concert 2026, held on the 2nd day of the 27th Founding Anniversary celebration and in observance of National Arts Month, became a memorable showcase of talent and creativity. The program featured performances from Pase Ballo, Pase Voci, Pase Teatro, and Pase Cuerdas.

The concert opened with dance performances from PASE Ballo, followed by a singing presentation from PASE Voci, and an acting performance from PASE Teat
label: artistry-and-unity-take-center-stage-at-pase-community-concert-2026

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/artistry-and-unity-take-center-stage-at-pase-community-concert-2026/]
E Ballo, followed by a singing presentation from PASE Voci, and an acting performance from PASE Teatro, with each group having its own spotlight to showcase their artistry and skills. It marked the first time that all PASE groups performed together on one stage while highlighting their unique talents individually.

The presentation celebrated unity, creativity, and the passion for the arts, making the community concert a meaningful part of the anniversary and National Arts Month celebration.

[#GC27thFoundingAnniversary](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/gc27thfoundinganniversary?__eep__=6&__cf

# GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Sunday, February 22, 2026**

The annual GC FUNdation RUN marked the start of Gordon College’s 27th Founding Anniversary, held on February 22, 2026, at the East Tapinac Oval Track.

The event gathered members of the Gordon College Faculty Association (GCFAS), administrative staff, students, alumni, and community participants under the pre-foundation day celebration motif, “Color Run.”

The program commenced with the assembly of participants, followed by an opening ceremony that featured
label: gc-color-run-kicks-off-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-color-run-kicks-off-27th-founding-anniversary/]
e program commenced with the assembly of participants, followed by an opening ceremony that featured welcome messages from Dr. Katherine Yap and Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano. A Zumba warm-up session, led by administrators, faculty members, and students from the Bachelor of Physical Education (BPED), energized attendees ahead of the race.

The run officially started at 6:30 AM as participants filled the track dressed in white shirts and athletic attire, ready to be splashed with vibrant colored powder and water.

More than a display of color and movement, this year’s FUNdation RUN is a colorful disp
label: gc-color-run-kicks-off-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-color-run-kicks-off-27th-founding-anniversary/]
 and water.

More than a display of color and movement, this year’s FUNdation RUN is a colorful display of the institution’s commitment to promoting wellness, unity, and community relations while marking the upcoming foundation celebration with lively and memorable moments. | via Sam Andrea R. # Unfiltered and Unforgettable: GC Talent Showcase Pushes Boundaries of Art and Advocacy

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Sunday, February 22, 2026**

The Gordon College (GC) Function Hall transforms into a cauldron of raw emotion and artistic flair as the Mr. & Ms. GC Talent Competition pushes contestants to their creative prowess on February 19, 2026.

MR. AND MS. GORDON COLLEGE TALENT PORTION

The atmosphere turned electric early on as Christian Mendoza and Marielle Denise Garcia from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) entered the stage. What began as
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
Denise Garcia from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) entered the stage. What began as a fluid, lyrical interpretative piece took a sharp, daring turn into a high-octane “sexy dance” routine.

The day took a turn toward the cinematic with a series of narrative-driven interpretative dances. College of Computer Studies (CCS) Representatives Latrell Justin Desmonte and Riza Joyce Fetalino delivered a sweeping romantic saga that left the room breathless, only to crush spirits with a calculated, heartbreaking finale.

Following in that somber vein, Paulo Tarrayo and Athena Faith Mangli
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
culated, heartbreaking finale.

Following in that somber vein, Paulo Tarrayo and Athena Faith Manglicmot, College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Candidates leaned heavily into the “tragic” aesthetic.

Breaking the dance-heavy flow, Juanito Dominador Jimenez and Charmyn KC Sierra, College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) students, delivered the night’s most socially conscious entry. Merging spoken word poetry with a gritty musical performance of Gloc-9’s “Upuan,” the pair took a firm political stand.

There was an energetic cultural display by College of Allied and Health Studies
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
rm political stand.

There was an energetic cultural display by College of Allied and Health Studies (CAHS) Andrei Vistada and Aura May Mesiano, which earned them a standing ovation for their professionalism.

The cultural celebration continued with Ronvasti Atenante and Precious Abigail Reforba from CEAS who brought the timeless elegance of the Kumakaret Dance to the hall.

Rounding out the talent showcase, Marc Anthony Acula and Samantha Macapagal (CBA) brought the ballroom flair to the GC Function Hall.

MX. GORDON COLLEGE TALENT PORTION

Confidence, creativity, and courage filled the Funct
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
ction Hall.

MX. GORDON COLLEGE TALENT PORTION

Confidence, creativity, and courage filled the Function Hall of Gordon College as candidates of the much-anticipated campus pageant—Mx. GC—showcased their talents on the morning of February 19, 2026.

Mx. Jin Tonx, from CEAS, gave a high-energy dance performance. Wearing an eye-catching costume, Mx. Jin Tonx radiated confidence and a commanding aura that immediately captured the audience’s attention.

Mx. Dave, another representative from CEAS department, shifted the mood with a heart-touching vocal performance with acting elements. Dressed in a
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
rtment, shifted the mood with a heart-touching vocal performance with acting elements. Dressed in a clown-inspired costume, Mx. Dave portrayed the painful realities of hidden loneliness—someone who smiles outwardly while struggling internally. Through expressive facial gestures and subtle dance movements, the final twist in the performance highlighted the silent struggles faced by LGBTQIA+ community who are often ridiculed yet carry unseen emotional burdens.

From the College of Business and Accountancy, Mx. Kally delivered an interpretative dance focusing on queerness and rejection. Inspired
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
ountancy, Mx. Kally delivered an interpretative dance focusing on queerness and rejection. Inspired by a song about unreciprocated love, the performance illustrated the painful experience of confessing only to hear “Pare, kaibigan lang kita.” The performance captured vulnerability and the emotional weight of being “frienzoned,” resonating strongly with the audience

The fourth candidate, Mx. Jat Ashanty, representing the CAHS department, showcased a dramatic acting performance accompanied by background music, addressing abuse against queeer individuals. The line “Sinong mapalad? Sino ang kaawa
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
kground music, addressing abuse against queeer individuals. The line “Sinong mapalad? Sino ang kaawa-awa?” echoed as a cry for mercy and justice. The skit also incorporated themes of life abroad as an Overseas Filipino Worker (OFW), connecting struggles of identity with displacement and survival. The emotional act prompted reflection and empathy from viewers.

Mx. MJ, from CHTM as well, then brought a wave of empowerment through a combination of dance, lip sync, and singing. Incorporating memes and sarcastic humor, the performance reclaimed stereotypes and celebrated resilience and oppression.
label: unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/unfiltered-and-unforgettable-gc-talent-showcase-pushes-boundaries-of-art-and-advocacy/]
and sarcastic humor, the performance reclaimed stereotypes and celebrated resilience and oppression. The audience eagerly joined in, singing along and turning the moment into a collective celebration of pride.

Closing the talent portion, Mx. Jeremy, another contender from the CEAS Department, delivered a fierce dance performance. With bold movements and striking stage presence, Mx. Jeremy ignited loud cheers from the crowd, ending the competition segment on a high note. | via Kana Maye Dueñas and Zena Alexandra Madulara/The FOREFRONT

# GCAT result, available within 3 days — ATU

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Saturday, February 21, 2026**

OLONGAPO CITY, Philippines—Admission and Testing Unit (ATU) Coordinator Christine Moselina said on Monday, February 16, that the Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) result will be available within three-working days after taking the exam.  
In an improvement over the previous week-long release period, the office has shortened the turnaround time of the admission test’s results to three days after examination.  
“Maximum na submission ng result [is] 3 days, ma-
label: gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu/]
 test’s results to three days after examination.  
“Maximum na submission ng result [is] 3 days, ma-u-upload na sa [Gordon College Admission (GCA)] portal nila ‘yong kanilang result. Ang waiting game diyan is ‘yong evaluation [from] coordinator,” Moselina said.  
She also noted that the new system reverses the usual flow: applicants must provide their details and requirements, including their Grade 11 records instead of Grade 12 for initial review before they can sign up for a new GCA account.  
“Before, ang dami kasing nagkakaroon ng problems with regard to the submission ng requirements. [Ma
label: gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu/]

“Before, ang dami kasing nagkakaroon ng problems with regard to the submission ng requirements. [May mga] nakapag-register pero ‘yong requirements kulang-kulang, so kami ang nahihirapan sa admissions.”  
However, applicants are still required to provide their Grade 12 records to their coordinators for evaluation at the time of their interview.  
As of February 20, with a week remaining until the expected end, the office has logged 3,930 registrations, compared to the 5,666 total applicants recorded last year.  
“GCAT natin is until June 30 pa, matagal-tagal pa. Madadagdagan pa ‘yan,” the coor
label: gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gcat-result-available-within-3-days-atu/]
rded last year.  
“GCAT natin is until June 30 pa, matagal-tagal pa. Madadagdagan pa ‘yan,” the coordinator said.  
The admission unit opened early online applications for a three-month window, running from November 27 through February 27. | via Keane Rafael Abdon, THE FOREFRONT

# The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**

The Performing Arts Student Ensemble (PASE) ignites a synergy of performances on the second day of the 27th Founding Anniversary on February 25, at East Tapinac Oval Grounds.

The first segment featured PASE Ballo’s contemporary dance performance to the song ‘Tala,’ touching the hearts of GCians with their moving steps.

Furthermore, PASE Voci’s chorale performance filled the air with harmonic voic
label: the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary/]
 their moving steps.

Furthermore, PASE Voci’s chorale performance filled the air with harmonic voices and unity, creating an ethereal atmosphere through their cover of ‘Bagong Umaga’ by Bayang Barrios.

The third segment was a play by PASE Teatro, showcasing relatable acting that highlighted the reality of social media’s impact on people’s lives.

In addition, PASE Ballo returned for another performance of a cultural inspired dance filled with enthusiasm and energy.

Moreover, the fifth segment was a collaboration between all PASE ensembles — Cuerdas, Teatro, and Voci. Storytelling and music
label: the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary/]
 was a collaboration between all PASE ensembles — Cuerdas, Teatro, and Voci. Storytelling and music united on a narrative of Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) acted by Teatro and set to Voci and Cuerdas’s rendition of ‘Pinoy Ako’ by Bamboo.

Following that is a performance from Teatro’s newest troupe, the collectives. The four-member rap group sang Filipino hip-hop songs that captured the culture of Pinoy music history. They performed covers of ‘Venus’ by Ron Henely, ‘Ikaw lang’ by Kiyo, and ‘Upuan’ by Gloc 9, bringing back nostalgic vibes to many GCians.

Finally, the last segment featured a c
label: the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary/]
an’ by Gloc 9, bringing back nostalgic vibes to many GCians.

Finally, the last segment featured a collaboration of Voci and Cuerdas. Following this is PASE Ballo’s dance performance, celebrating Olongapo’s culture and the jeepney tradition. The performance transitioned to Cuerdas and Voci, who performed a reggae song.

The event proudly celebrated National Arts Month this February, showcasing the diverse talents and artistry of GCians. Through various performances, the students highlighted their creativity and passion for the arts, demonstrating the vibrant cultural landscape of the instituti
label: the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary/]
r creativity and passion for the arts, demonstrating the vibrant cultural landscape of the institution. It was a testament to the rich artistic expression and talent that GCians have, making it a memorable celebration of arts and culture, leaving a lasting impression on the audiences at the 27th founding anniversary. | via Lei Ydah Lasala Ledesma / The FOREFRONT

# GC ALAB 2026: Connecting Job Seekers to Opportunities on GC’s 27th Anniversary

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**

In celebration of the 27th Founding Anniversary, the GC ALAB Job Fair was successfully conducted on February 25, 2026, at Ayala Malls Harbor Point – Activity Center from 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM.

The event was spearheaded by the Public Employment Service Office (PESO) in collaboration with Gordon College, particularly under the Community Extension Unit and the Office of Student Welfare and Services, as part of the institution’s c
label: gc-alab-2026-connecting-job-seekers-to-opportunities-on-gcs-27th-anniversary

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-alab-2026-connecting-job-seekers-to-opportunities-on-gcs-27th-anniversary/]
munity Extension Unit and the Office of Student Welfare and Services, as part of the institution’s commitment to serving its stakeholders by providing accessible employment opportunities.

A total of 30 participating companies joined the job fair, offering various career opportunities for job seekers and fresh graduates.

This meaningful activity was made possible through the partnership of Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), SBMA Labor, Ayala Malls Harbor Point, JPMAP, SMAP, and the College of Business and Accountancy.

The GC ALAB Job Fair reflects Gordon College’s dedication to commu
label: gc-alab-2026-connecting-job-seekers-to-opportunities-on-gcs-27th-anniversary

ege of Business and Accountancy.

The GC ALAB Job Fair reflects Gordon College’s dedication to community extension and workforce development.

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

Gordon College proudly received Certificates of Accreditation for its 21 ALCUCOA-accredited programs during the NNQAA 2nd Quality Assurance Summit and Conferment Ceremony for Accredited Programs and Institutions 2026, with the theme “Responsible Future: Quality Assurance in Action.”

The recognition was conferred by the National Network of Quality Assurance Agencies (NNQAA), Inc., led by Chairman Dr. Raymundo P. Arcega, during the s

# GC marks second year in One Billion Rising movement to end violence against women and girls

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Monday, February 16, 2026**

Gordon College marked its second consecutive year of participation in the global campaign One Billion Rising through a public awareness activity themed “Rise for our bodies, our Earth, our Future,” held at Ayala Malls Harbor Point on February 14.  
The event brought together students and mall-goers in a collective call to stand against violence toward women and girls while promoting empowerment and social responsib
label: gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls/]
ll to stand against violence toward women and girls while promoting empowerment and social responsibility.  
Established in 2012, One Billion Rising has developed into an international movement recognized for mobilizing communities across the world to advocate for the elimination of gender-based violence.  
The initiative is grounded in global data from the United Nations indicating that approximately one in three women experience violence at some point in their lives, representing more than a billion women and girls worldwide.  
The activity was led by the Institutional Planning, Development,
label: gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls/]
illion women and girls worldwide.  
The activity was led by the Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs (IPDEA) through the Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit in partnership with the National Service Training Program (NSTP) and Ayala Malls Inc., with the goal of strengthening public awareness and encouraging community participation in the advocacy.  
One of the program’s highlights was a flash mob performance by Pase Ballo and SHAPES, who led a mass dance to the One Billion Rising anthem, Break the Chain, symbolizing unity and resistance.  
Mr. Joseph Atencio, head o
label: gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls/]
llion Rising anthem, Break the Chain, symbolizing unity and resistance.  
Mr. Joseph Atencio, head of the DEI Unit, emphasized that violence against women and girls does not only occur in public spaces but is often present within homes and personal relationships.  
“Bukod sa awareness na gusto nating ipakalat, gusto nating maging mapagmatyag ‘yung mga tao. The purpose of One Billion Rising is truly to end violence against women and girls. There can be no sustainable development without women’s empowerment,” he remarked. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara/The FOREFRONT

Photos by Ellisha Dawn & Angel

# 3 studes earn top spots in CFMS and CMMS exams; GC produces new CPACE-certified undergrads

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, December 23, 2025**

Francis Jerome De Jesus and Belynda Apog, 4th-year Bachelor of Science in Business Administration Major in Financial Management students, ranked 4th and 5th, respectively, in their assessment examinations. Meanwhile, Marketing Management major Michaella Soliven secured the 10th spot of her own.

In a post, the Center for Professional Advancement and Continuing Education, Inc. (CPACE Philippines) announced the passe
label: 3-studes-earn-top-spots-in-cfms-and-cmms-exams-gc-produces-new-cpace-certified-undergrads

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/3-studes-earn-top-spots-in-cfms-and-cmms-exams-gc-produces-new-cpace-certified-undergrads/]
 for Professional Advancement and Continuing Education, Inc. (CPACE Philippines) announced the passers of the Batch 25 Certified Financial Management Specialist (CFMS)® and Certified Marketing Management Specialist (CMMS)® assessment examinations on Thursday, December 18, and Friday, December 19, respectively.

De Jesus, 90.42%, and Apog, 90%, both broke into the Top 10 for the CFMS® assessment exam. On the flip side, Soliven cleared the CMMS® assessment with a rating of 86.67%.

Furthermore, 78 additional Financial Management from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) students cleared
label: 3-studes-earn-top-spots-in-cfms-and-cmms-exams-gc-produces-new-cpace-certified-undergrads

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/3-studes-earn-top-spots-in-cfms-and-cmms-exams-gc-produces-new-cpace-certified-undergrads/]
 additional Financial Management from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) students cleared the assessment, bringing the institutional passing rate to 76.70%, while the results also saw one more taker pass the CMMS® exam.

CPACE Philippines totaled an overall passing rate of 69.88% and 80% for CFMS® and CMMS® examinations, respectively.

The assessment examinations were conducted via online and in-person formats, including Gordon College (GC) – Annex Bldg., from November 28 through December 13. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, December 10, 2025**

Today, the College was honored by the Commission on Higher Education for our outstanding achievements in global rankings, sustainability, ASEAN awareness, and internationalization during the Internationalization Champions of Nation-Building and Sustainability (ICONS) Awards 2025, held on December 10, 2025 (Wednesday), from 1:00 PM to 9:00 PM at the Philippine International Conve
label: gc-recoginzed-as-internationalization-champion-icons-for-its-performance-in-the-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025

[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-recoginzed-as-internationalization-champion-icons-for-its-performance-in-the-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025/]
held on December 10, 2025 (Wednesday), from 1:00 PM to 9:00 PM at the Philippine International Convention Center (PICC) in Pasay City.

The College was recognized for its high-impact sustainability initiatives, commendable performance in international rankings and ratings, pioneering efforts to advance internationalization, and valuable contributions to United Nation’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

This recognition is the result of our Internationalization initiative, based on the released ranking results for this year in the Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025.

# Ballo and GCians Capture 1st and 2nd Runner-ups at Cultural Competition

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, December 23, 2025**

Gordon College’s (GC) Performing Arts Students Ensemble (PASE) Ballo and other students secured the 1st and 2nd runner-up titles in the Barangay Cultural Competition at Gordon Avenue, on December 20, at SM City Downtown.

Members of PASE Ballo and other GCians competed in their respective groups, representing Barangay Pag-asa and Barangay West Tapinac.

Barangay Pag-asa performed their Sagudsod de Mano, which led them to be placed as
label: ballo-and-gcians-capture-1st-and-2nd-runner-ups-at-cultural-competition

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ballo-and-gcians-capture-1st-and-2nd-runner-ups-at-cultural-competition/]
gay West Tapinac.

Barangay Pag-asa performed their Sagudsod de Mano, which led them to be placed as the first runner-up.

Meanwhile, Barangay West Tapinac earned their place as the second runner-up after grooving their way through their Inalimango performance.

Despite the wet roads due to the rain, the participants from the institution still showed their grace and effort in their renditions, embracing the Filipino culture.

The folk dance competition was a part of the Olongapo City Fiesta Celebration. | via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

# CEAS Pins 269 Pre-service Educators in 18th Convocation Ceremony

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, December 10, 2025**

SMX Convention Center — The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) marked a milestone in the journey of its future educators during the 18th Pinning and Candle Lighting Ceremony last December 3, Wednesday, as the student teachers became full fledged practice teachers.

This year’s cohort included a total of 269 student teachers across various programs: 22 from Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd), 17 from Bachelor of Ea
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
oss various programs: 22 from Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd), 17 from Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECED), 50 from Bachelor of Elementary Education (BEED), 42 from Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd), and 138 from Bachelor of Secondary Education (BSEd) who are deployed in various public and private schools across Olongapo City.

In his welcome remarks, Dr. Darwin Paguio, OIC-Dean of the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS), emphasized Gordon College’s commitment to transferring theory learned in the classroom onto the field. He highlighted how CEAS’s educ
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
tment to transferring theory learned in the classroom onto the field. He highlighted how CEAS’s education programs ensure that every deployed student teacher is academically prepared within the “four corners of the classroom” and fully ready to apply these theories once they step into real teaching environments.

Following this is the ceremonial passing of the Torch and Book of Wisdom, carried by Mr. Kristoffer Obispo and Ms. Juanalyn Olaguer, respectively. “The torch has been an important symbol since early civilization,” said Dr. Catherine Mobo as the torch is lit, explaining that it embodie
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
l since early civilization,” said Dr. Catherine Mobo as the torch is lit, explaining that it embodies the “light of tomorrow” that educators carry into the lives of learners. Meanwhile, Ms. Agnes Melo presented the Book of Wisdom, reminding the future educators that “its pages hold not only theories but the hopes and futures of learners we are called to guide.”

Delivering the keynote speech, Ms. Ilynne Samonte, CESE, Assistant Schools Division Superintendent, shared five heartfelt “pabaon” from her own internship journey at Philippine Normal University (PNU). She shared lessons she hopes the
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
 her own internship journey at Philippine Normal University (PNU). She shared lessons she hopes the student teachers will carry into their practicum from the significance of being on time to the importance of taking care of oneself.

She also reminded the attendees of their future roles in public service, expressing her confidence that many of the candidates would one day serve in the Department of Education: “Most of you, if not all of you, will join us at DepEd sooner or later… Sabi nga, hindi pwede ang ‘pwede na.’ Ultimately, that will be your contribution to your community and your country
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
 pwede ang ‘pwede na.’ Ultimately, that will be your contribution to your community and your country.”

The 269 student teachers were ceremoniously pinned by their guardians with an official teacher’s nameplate. Their designated resource teachers — tenured teachers in their deployed school who have guided them — also passed the flames of their candle onto the wicks of their student teacher’s candle.

Solemn tears were shed at SMX as an audio-visual presentation was shown with photos from their college journey set to Ben & Ben’s hit song “Saranggola”. “Sa’n man tayo hipan ng amihan, hindi ipagp
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
ollege journey set to Ben & Ben’s hit song “Saranggola”. “Sa’n man tayo hipan ng amihan, hindi ipagpapalit ang pagkakaibigan,” the song played as numerous iconic moments through the years flashed on screen.

Emotions further deepened as the student teachers sang a musical tribute of “MAPA” by SB19 as they gifted a rose to their parents whose support made the milestone possible.

The ceremony concluded with closing remarks from Mr. Samuel Felip D. Fastidio, Field Study Supervisor and Program Chair of the BPEd Program, who congratulated the candidates and encouraged them to carry their symbols—t
label: ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/ceas-pins-269-pre-service-educators-in-18th-convocation-ceremony/]
r of the BPEd Program, who congratulated the candidates and encouraged them to carry their symbols—torch, book, and candle—with a purpose as they step into the teaching profession as Gordon College’s newest teaching interns.

After hours of classroom observation in their field study, the 269 student teachers who are deployed in various schools will now formally step into their practicum as they take over their respective school classrooms. | via Love Lorraine Acedo & Lance Isaac Leon, The FOREFRONT

# GC releases statement over alleged P19-million overpayment

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Monday, January 5, 2026**

“The College wishes to clarify that funds received under the Free Higher Education Program are meticulously allocated and budgeted which undergoes strict audit by appropriate national government agencies. All collections approved were allocated transparently for the operations and development of the college,” Gordon College (GC) stated.

Following the publication of a Commission on Audit (COA) report cited by Bilyonaryo News Channel, GC released an
label: gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment/]
 publication of a Commission on Audit (COA) report cited by Bilyonaryo News Channel, GC released an official statement addressing the alleged P19-million overpayment under the Free Higher Education (FHE) Program.

The institution clarifies that all funds received were meticulously allocated and budgeted, emphasizing that all approved funds were used transparently for the institution’s operations and development.

According to the COA report, there are three (3) Local Universities and Colleges (LUCs) with ineligibilities and excessive fees due to loss in control mechanism of the Commission on H
label: gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment/]
UCs) with ineligibilities and excessive fees due to loss in control mechanism of the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Central Office and Unified Students Financial Assistance System for Tertiary Education (UniFAST) Billing Unit, amounting to more than P295-million.

The three institutions identified were the Tangub City Global College (TCGC), Pamantasan ng Cabuyao (PNC), and Gordon College (GC).

For GC, COA reported a total amount of P19,260,800 in questioned fees. The audit findings pointed to a mismatch between GC’s definition of a laboratory room and COA’s definition of an Audio-Visua
label: gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment/]
ed to a mismatch between GC’s definition of a laboratory room and COA’s definition of an Audio-Visual Room (AVR).

Meanwhile, PNC listed an overpayment amounting to P55,818,690, which focuses on the leadership training, student partnerships, student publication and newsletters, and remedial programs under the Development category—being the highest recipient.

On the other hand, TCGC recorded the highest alleged overpayment at P220,706,895, with cultural fees accounting for the largest portion of the amount.

Furthermore, COA noted that this was not the first time CHED had been called out for o
label: gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment/]
 the amount.

Furthermore, COA noted that this was not the first time CHED had been called out for overpayments under the FHE Program.

Audit observations from 2021 to 2022 already pointed to excess budgeting, while the 2024 report cited lapses in verification processes of the CHED Central Office and UniFAST Secretariat, resulting in the continued cycle of observations and disallowances since fiscal year 2022.

As part of its general recommendations, COA directed the three institutions to return the overpayments made by CHED, or to avail themselves of other appropriate modes of settlement of a
label: gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment/]
 the overpayments made by CHED, or to avail themselves of other appropriate modes of settlement of accounts in accordance with the existing laws and regulations.

In response, GC reiterated its commitment to accountability and transparency, emphasizing its willingness to cooperate with auditing authorities and comply with lawful processes. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Monday, December 22, 2025**

The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) beams with pride as it yields 39 Licensed Professional Teachers (LPTs) for the Elementary level and 161 new LPTs for the Secondary level at this year’s Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT)

Gordon College (GC) once again surpassed the national passing rates in the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) conducted on September 21 and November 30, 202
label: blept-2025-yields-200-new-gc-produced-professional-teachers

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/blept-2025-yields-200-new-gc-produced-professional-teachers/]
censure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) conducted on September 21 and November 30, 2025.

For the Elementary Level, 39 out of 49 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, resulting in a passing rate of 79.59% against a national passing rate of 51.04%.

Meanwhile, for the Secondary Level, 161 out of 177 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, posting an overall passing rate of 90.96%, with 50% of second-takers also passing.

Overall, 76,696 out of 122,528 examinees nationwide passed the September–November 2025 BLEPT, representing a national passing rate of 65.04% for the seconda
label: blept-2025-yields-200-new-gc-produced-professional-teachers

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/blept-2025-yields-200-new-gc-produced-professional-teachers/]
ed the September–November 2025 BLEPT, representing a national passing rate of 65.04% for the secondary level.

Notably, the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) was rescheduled in Regions I, II and Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) due to inclement weather.

The examinations for the said regions were conducted on November 30. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# HISTORY IN THE MAKING: GCIANS CONQUER THE GLOBAL GAMING STAGE!

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, February 17, 2026**

The legend returns, and our very own alumni are leading the charge! Gordon College is bursting with pride as we celebrate the official launch of God of War: Sons of Sparta on the PS5!

This isn’t just a win for the gaming world; it’s a massive milestone for our institution. We are incredibly honored to recognize the brilliant minds from our BSEMC and BSCS programs who collaborated with Santa Monica Studio, Mega Cat Studios, and PlayStation to
label: history-in-the-making-gcians-conquer-the-global-gaming-stage

content:

MC and BSCS programs who collaborated with Santa Monica Studio, Mega Cat Studios, and PlayStation to bring this retro-inspired masterpiece to life.

A huge round of applause for our talented Alumni:

BSEMC ALUMNI:

* Daniele Victor R. Marilag
* Marianne Silver
* Mark Angelo A. Quindara
* Roselle Gaw

BSCS ALUMNI:

# TF SECURES LHEPC SPOT

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Saturday, February 14, 2026**

The Forefront journos once again demonstrated their grit and skills in campus journalism, securing several awards at the 24th Regional Higher Education Press Conference (RHEPC), held at Palm Cabanas Resort, Arayat, Pampanga, from February 11 to 13.

Two of the publication’s outgoing executive members, John Patrick Mateo (Editor-in-Chief) and Lance Isaac Leon (Associate Editor), were recognized as Outstanding Tertiary Campus Journalists (OTCJ) in Region III.

In the group category,
label: tf-secures-lhepc-spot

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tf-secures-lhepc-spot/]
recognized as Outstanding Tertiary Campus Journalists (OTCJ) in Region III.

In the group category, the pub’s entry for Opening Billboard ranked 4th Place overall.

In individual categories, delegates from the publication bagged nine (9) awards as listed below.

Angelique Jose

1st Place – Traditional Literary Graphics Illustration (English)

Lance Isaac Leon

2nd Place – Editorial Writing (English)

3rd Place – Opinion Writing (English)

John Patrick Mateo

3rd Place – Devcom Writing (Filipino)

John Christian Rocero

3rd Place – Photojournalism (English)

Mary Isobelle Arcilla

5th Place – T
label: tf-secures-lhepc-spot

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tf-secures-lhepc-spot/]


John Christian Rocero

3rd Place – Photojournalism (English)

Mary Isobelle Arcilla

5th Place – Traditional Comic Strip Drawing (English)

6th Place – Traditional Editorial Cartooning (English)

Keane Rafael Abdon

8th Place – News Writing (English)

Mikhaela Sagun

10th Place – Poetry Writing (English)

Overall, The Forefront is the 11th Highest Pointer in individual categories against 42 participating college publications in Central Luzon.

Additionally, Keane Rafael Abdon is the newly elected division coordinator of Olongapo City for the Young Journalist Association of Region III (YJAR-I
label: tf-secures-lhepc-spot

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tf-secures-lhepc-spot/]
ted division coordinator of Olongapo City for the Young Journalist Association of Region III (YJAR-III).

Five campus journalists from the publication, who garnered top 5 placements in their respective categories, will advance to the 21st Luzon-Wide Higher Education Press Conference (LHEPC) in Tuguegarao City, Cagayan, next month to compete in eight categories. | via Mikhaela Sagun and Lance Isaac Leon, THE FOREFRONT

Photos by Guiller T. Marila and John Christian J. Rocero

# Initial Presentation of the Project METRO

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Thursday, January 29, 2026**

Gordon College – College of Computer Studies (GC-CCS), in partnership with the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) – Zambales, successfully conducted the initial product presentation of LPCC Ikot Palengke and Fair Trade Law Monitoring activities under PROJECT METRO: Monitoring Enforcement & Tracking Resource Online on January 29, 2026.

The presentation was piloted by OneDev, a team of third-year BSIT student developers—Marina Camaso, Paulo Cordova, Nikolai Ne
label: initial-presentation-of-the-project-metro

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/initial-presentation-of-the-project-metro/]
ted by OneDev, a team of third-year BSIT student developers—Marina Camaso, Paulo Cordova, Nikolai Nepomuceno, Earl Romeo Ordovez, Aaron Paul Sario, and Seann Patrick Tamondong—who showcased a prototype of a digital system designed to enhance government operations across province-wide public markets and retail establishments. The system aims to strengthen consumer protection, enforce fair trade laws, and improve efficient public service delivery through accurate data collection and monitoring.

The activity was attended by Gordon College administrators, headed by Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College
label: initial-presentation-of-the-project-metro

content:

Casela-Abarintos, Vice President for Administration and Finance; Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, Vice President for Institutional Planning and External Affairs; Dr. Ediric D. Gadia, Vice President for Research Development and Community Extension Services; Dr. Precious De Castro, Coordinator of the International Affairs Unit; Prof. Ronnie D. Luy, Dean of the College of Computer Studies; Prof. Armilyn T. Martinez, As
label: initial-presentation-of-the-project-metro

rs Unit; Prof. Ronnie D. Luy, Dean of the College of Computer Studies; Prof. Armilyn T. Martinez, Assistant Dean of the College of Computer Studies; and Prof. Reynaldo G. Bautista Jr., Program Coordinator of the Bachelor of Science in Information Technology. Also present were officials from DTI Zambales, led by Mr. Neil John Fabay, Division Chief of the Consumer Protection Division, along with Ms. Marilou Benonto and Ms. Melody Tangonan, Senior Trade Industry Development Specialists.

This initiative highlights Gordon College’s commitment to innovation and technology-driven governance, emphasi
label: initial-presentation-of-the-project-metro

ative highlights Gordon College’s commitment to innovation and technology-driven governance, emphasizing its role in advancing the institution’s global technology and general assistance initiatives.

# GC-SSC benchmarks student leadership and governance among HEIs

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, November 19, 2025**

In line with the commitment of the Student Council in upholding the inherent rights and cardinal necessities of the academic community, the 𝗚𝗼𝗿𝗱𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗲 – 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝘂𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗹 (GC-SSC) conducts a benchmarking activity on best practices, strategies for student leadership development, and organizational dynamics on November 17-18, 2025, at 𝘾𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙧𝙖𝙡 𝙇𝙪𝙯𝙤𝙣 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮, 𝙏𝙖𝙧𝙡𝙖𝙘 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮, 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝘽𝙪𝙡𝙖𝙘𝙖𝙣 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮. This initiat
label: gc-ssc-benchmarks-student-leadership-and-governance-among-heis

content:

 𝘾𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙧𝙖𝙡 𝙇𝙪𝙯𝙤𝙣 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮, 𝙏𝙖𝙧𝙡𝙖𝙘 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮, 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝘽𝙪𝙡𝙖𝙘𝙖𝙣 𝙎𝙩𝙖𝙩𝙚 𝙐𝙣𝙞𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙨𝙞𝙩𝙮. This initiative is conducted to reaffirm the commitment of the Student Council in protecting and advocating the rights of every student of Gordon College.

# WAY TO GO! GC on its 3-PEAT VICTORY

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, October 29, 2025**

WAY TO GO! Congratulations to our College on its 3-PEAT VICTORY, emerging as CHAMPION at the Philippine Statistics Quiz – Provincial Elimination! The event was held on October 28, 2025, organized by the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) Zambales.

We are so proud of our students who are now Qualifiers for the Regional Competition on November 6, 2025, at the Orchid Garden Resort, Pampanga:

1. Andrei Fredeluces

2. Sophia Alaiza Esnaldo

3. Rachelle Andrea Rivera
label: way-to-go-gc-on-its-3-peat-victory

content:

arden Resort, Pampanga:

1. Andrei Fredeluces

2. Sophia Alaiza Esnaldo

3. Rachelle Andrea Rivera

This incredible achievement was guided by our dedicated coaches, Mr. St. Lawrence Abogadie and Mr. Ryan Justin Fruto.

Special thanks as well to our BSEd in Mathematics Program Chair, Dr. Katherine D. Yap, for her unwavering support and for representing the College on behalf of our College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano.

# Gordon College receives Academic Excellence Award 2025

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, December 9, 2025**

Gordon College receives the prestigious Academic Excellence Award from ALCUCOA for significantly demonstrating excellence in education through its outstanding performance in the recent Licensure Examinations conducted by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC).

The award specifically recognizes the college’s exceptional results, which include:

1. November 2025 Midwifery Licensure Examination: Achieving a 100% passing rate for first-time takers,
label: gordon-college-receives-academic-excellence-award-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gordon-college-receives-academic-excellence-award-2025/]
 November 2025 Midwifery Licensure Examination: Achieving a 100% passing rate for first-time takers, far exceeding the national average. This perfect score solidifies Gordon College’s reputation for producing highly competent health professionals.

2. May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE): Gordon College earns national recognition for producing top-ranking examinees.

Top 9 Topnotchers: Martin Jorge Bernal Alcantara and Loren Mirador Mistica

3. May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE): Also producing a top-ranking examinee:

Top 8 Topnotcher: Rose Sheilla Mae
label: gordon-college-receives-academic-excellence-award-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gordon-college-receives-academic-excellence-award-2025/]
sure Examination (PNLE): Also producing a top-ranking examinee:

Top 8 Topnotcher: Rose Sheilla Mae D. Domingo

This remarkable achievement in both the Midwifery and Nursing licensure exams underscores the effectiveness of Gordon College’s rigorous academic programs, the dedication of its faculty, and the quality of its students.

With this significant recognition, the college is reinvigorated and eager to pursue its commitment to providing quality higher education and continuing its mission to produce competent, industry-ready alumni who are well-equipped to excel in their respective fields.

# GC ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**

Nagsimula na ang tatlong-araw na akreditasyon ng Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) sa Gordon College.

Ngayong taon, ang Bachelor of Science in Business Administration (BSBA) Major in Marketing Management ay nangangarap makamit ang Level III Accreditation.

Samantala, ang Bachelor of Science in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary E
label: gc-alcucoa-accreditation-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-alcucoa-accreditation-2025/]
 Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Filipino (BSEd-Fil), at Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Science (BSEd-Sci) ay target naman ang Level II Accreditation.

Bukod dito, ang Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd) at Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd) ay nominado bilang mga kandidato para sa akreditasyon ngayong taon.

Suportado ito ni Mayor [Atty. Rolen Paulino Jr.](https://www.facebook.com/mayorlenjpaulino?__cft__[0]=AZU3QvTqhQlkmJYMC3O-Ag6rBVcz3QYgae7wuVrFTGSN7kZ89KmJdl5ubPj8hN-KEPPY6EcfTQTGxY

# GC produces 9 new CPAs; yields 39.13% pass rate in October CPALE

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, November 4, 2025**

Nine alumni from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) of Gordon College (GC) were among the passers of the October Certified Public Accountant Licensure Exam (CPALE) per Professional Regulatory Board of Accountancy, on November 4.

GC garnered an overall passing rate of 39.13% from 9 passers out of 23 takers, surpassing the national passing rate of 34.02%. These constitute the 3,460 newly registered Certified Public Accountants (CPA
label: gc-produces-9-new-cpas-yields-39-13-pass-rate-in-october-cpale

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-produces-9-new-cpas-yields-39-13-pass-rate-in-october-cpale/]
assing rate of 34.02%. These constitute the 3,460 newly registered Certified Public Accountants (CPA).

Comprising this are three first-time takers and six repeater examinees.

In addition, Mike Ronnel Castro of Pampanga State University (for. Don Honorario Ventura State University) topped the examination with a 91.83% rating.

Bicol University – Daraga is the lone institution in the list of top-performing schools with 50 or more examinees with an 84.85% passing rate.

The licensure exam was conducted from October 24 to 26. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# WARRIORS’ CARNAGE: GC Bags Second Championship Streak at LCUAA National Games

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Saturday, October 25, 2025**

After six days of grueling competition, the Gordon College (GC) Warriors are hailed as the ultimate victors of the 14th Local Colleges and Universities Athletic Association (LCUAA) National Games, marking GC’s second winning streak as the overall champion.

Across several categories, the Warriors collectively bagged 31 gold, 22 silver, and 24 bronze medals across their LCUAA stint, a resounding sweep above overall 2nd place Kol
label: warriors-carnage-gc-bags-second-championship-streak-at-lcuaa-national-games

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/warriors-carnage-gc-bags-second-championship-streak-at-lcuaa-national-games/]
ilver, and 24 bronze medals across their LCUAA stint, a resounding sweep above overall 2nd place Kolehiyo ng Lungsod ng Lipa’s (KLL) 23 gold, 18 silver, and 21 bronze medal tally.

Meanwhile, the overall 3rd place title was claimed by Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig (PLP) with 15 gold, 13 silver, and 15 bronze medals across their run.

Furthermore, GC brought home championship plaques in the swimming, dancesport, and taekwondo categories awarded today, October 25, at the CSJDM Sports Complex. The Olongapo-reps were also awarded plaques for 1st runner-up in badminton, chess, and men’s futsal, as
label: warriors-carnage-gc-bags-second-championship-streak-at-lcuaa-national-games

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/warriors-carnage-gc-bags-second-championship-streak-at-lcuaa-national-games/]
 Olongapo-reps were also awarded plaques for 1st runner-up in badminton, chess, and men’s futsal, as well as; 2nd runner-up in athletics.

The Warriors are set to defend their winning streak at next year’s national games at Binoloan, Pangasinan, hosted by the University of Eastern Pangasinan (UEP). | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

**Friday, November 21, 2025**

Gordon College (GC) has shown its strong performance in Customs Administration with the release of the Customs Brokers Licensure Examination (CUBLE) 2025 results.

# BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, November 11, 2025**

GORDON COLLEGE FUNCTION HALL MAIN BLDG—In an effort to strengthen transparency and communication between the student body and the administration, various students and representatives from different organizations voice out their concerns and proposed solutions on pressing campus issues that affect their daily experiences on the Town Hall Meeting, spearheaded by the Supreme Student Council (SSC) of Gordon College (GC) on November 4
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
 Hall Meeting, spearheaded by the Supreme Student Council (SSC) of Gordon College (GC) on November 4, at Gordon College Function Hall Main Bldg.

Among the major issues discussed were concerns with the disappearance of trash bins, restroom malfunctions, and the absence of student lounges, which reflect the everyday struggles of GCians that often go unnoticed. While these may seem like small matters, they collectively define the comfort, cleanliness, and morale of the student body.

Meanwhile, other pressing matters, such as the condition of the main and annex buildings and inconsistent impleme
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
her pressing matters, such as the condition of the main and annex buildings and inconsistent implementation of the dress code, point to deeper issues of transparency, inclusivity, and student welfare.

DISAPPEARANCE OF TRASH BINS: CLEANLINESS AND WASTE MANAGEMENT ISSUES RAISED

Campus cleanliness had been a pressing issue among students following the recent disappearance of trash bins across campus. Students reported that many areas, including restrooms, now lack accessible waste bins, leading to increased littering and cleanliness issues.

Participants suggest restoration of the trash bins th
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
o increased littering and cleanliness issues.

Participants suggest restoration of the trash bins throughout the campus to promote cleanliness and for students’ easier access, emphasizing that this could help maintain a clean environment around the campus.

BUILDING FUNCTIONALITY: CALLS FOR BETTER FACILITIES BOTH IN THE MAIN AND ANNEX BUILDINGS

Moreover, the Annex Building, which accommodates a growing number of classes, points to another major concern. Students described poor ventilation, unstable internet connectivity, and a lack of proper equipment—including the sound system in the Functio
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
able internet connectivity, and a lack of proper equipment—including the sound system in the Function Room—as recurring problems that lessen their productivity.

Furthermore, students also expressed frustrations over the lack of equipment in the Main Building, such as practicums and simulations.

The SSC noted the importance of these concerns and emphasized the need for facility upgrades to ensure a more conducive academic environment, emphasizing that as Gordon College aims to be a university, infrastructure and facility improvements must keep pace with academic demands.

DRESS CODE VIOLATION
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
infrastructure and facility improvements must keep pace with academic demands.

DRESS CODE VIOLATIONS: STUDENTS CALL FOR CONSISTENCY AND CLARITY

One of the most discussed topics was the implementation of the college’s dress code policy. Several students expressed confusion over inconsistent enforcement across departments, citing instances of inconsistency and a lack of clear communication throughout the student body.

“Minsan po navi-violation kami kahit appropriate po ‘yung suot namin [na uniform],” one Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Student expressed.

Participants also n
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
orm],” one Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Student expressed.

Participants also noted that some students are reprimanded for minor violations while others are not held to the same standard.

“Naka-org shirt po ako then ni-question po ako kung bakit [naka-org shirt]. Nag-explain po ako na may event, pero may nauna po sakin na naka-civilian [pero] ‘di po siya ni-question,” another student from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) shared.

In the latter part of the meeting, Gcians requested clearer guidelines and a more uniform implementation across departments to he
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
ting, Gcians requested clearer guidelines and a more uniform implementation across departments to help promote both discipline and inclusivity within the campus community, and avoid misunderstanding and unequal treatment.

STUDENT LOUNGE, RESTROOM, AND OTHER CONCERNS

The functionality and cleanliness of restrooms were also brought up as a long-standing concern. Some facilities were reported to have malfunctioning faucets and dysfunctional doors, toilets, and bidets. Students cite concerns for regular maintenance checks to ensure comfort and hygiene for all.

On the other hand, Participants al
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
egular maintenance checks to ensure comfort and hygiene for all.

On the other hand, Participants also voiced out frustrations over the lack of student lounges and rest areas where they can relax, study, or collaborate during vacant periods. Many pointed out that the few available classrooms require permission to be used, forcing students to stay in hallways or stairwells in between classes.

One student from CHTM proposed designating student lounges on each floor to provide space for students as a resolution, citing that this would provide comfort for students.

The SSC assured attendees that
label: basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/basura-pasilidad-uniporme-students-address-concerns-in-town-hall-meeting/]
s a resolution, citing that this would provide comfort for students.

The SSC assured attendees that all issues and recommendations raised will be compiled and formally addressed to the college administration for further deliberation with the BIBINGKA project, held on November 7, Friday.

The council also promised its commitment to continue facilitating such dialogues to ensure that students’ voices are not only heard but also acted upon. | via Love Lorraine Acedo/The FOREFRONT

# HON. ROLEN C. PAULINO JR. RECEIVES THE MODEL LOCAL CHIEF EXECUTIVE AWARD FOR 2025 FROM ALCUCOA

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Tuesday, December 9, 2025**

Huge congratulations to our visionary City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., who was conferred the prestigious Model Local Chief Executive Award for 2025 by the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) on December 9, 2025, at the Diamond Hotel in Manila.

Our City Mayor received this top honor in recognition of his exemplary leadership and outstanding commitment to adv
label: hon-rolen-c-paulino-jr-receives-the-model-local-chief-executive-award-for-2025-from-alcucoa

[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/hon-rolen-c-paulino-jr-receives-the-model-local-chief-executive-award-for-2025-from-alcucoa/]
received this top honor in recognition of his exemplary leadership and outstanding commitment to advancing quality and accessible higher education in Olongapo. His administration was specifically lauded for successfully elevating academic standards and modernizing campus infrastructure to secure a globally competitive educational landscape for the city’s constituents.

# GC Welcomes 5 new Registered Midwifes, Perfect passing in November MLE

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, November 19, 2025**

Five alumni from the College of Allied Health and Services (CAHS) of Gordon College (GC) passed the Midwives Licensure Exam (MLE) according to the Professional Regulation Commission Board.

GC achieved a perfect passing rate of 100% with 5 passers out of 5 examinees, once again surpassing the rate of last year’s results of 93.48% and the national rate of 57.60%. They constitute the 2,152 newly registered midwives.

All are first-tim
label: gc-welcomes-5-new-registered-midwifes-perfect-passing-in-november-mle

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-welcomes-5-new-registered-midwifes-perfect-passing-in-november-mle/]
the national rate of 57.60%. They constitute the 2,152 newly registered midwives.

All are first-time takers of this year’s exam.

Additionally, Tagaloan Community College is the top-performing school in the licensure exam with a perfect passing rate from 85 examinees of the school.

Meanwhile, Princesa Dumale Talamayan, this year’s board topnotcher, from Palawan State University achieved a 94.00%.

The licensure exam was held on November 8-9 and 16. | via Roi Chester B. Manio/The FOREFRONT

# GC welcomes ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**

The three-day accreditation kicks off as the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) arrives today, November 26, at the Gordon College (GC) Main Bldg.

This year, the Bachelor of Science in Business Administration (BSBA) Major in Marketing Management vies for Level III Accreditation.

Furthermore, the Bachelor of Science in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education
label: gc-welcomes-alcucoa-accreditation-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-welcomes-alcucoa-accreditation-2025/]
in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Filipino (BSEd-Fil), and Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Science (BSEd-Sci) are aiming for the Level II Accreditation.

Meanwhile, Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd) and Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd) are now candidates for this year’s accreditation.

The accreditation 2025 is set from November 26-28, at the Function Hall, Gordon College Main Bldg. | via John Patrick Mateo/ The FOREFRONT

# GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Wednesday, December 3, 2025**

The three-day program comes to an end as Gordon College (GC) conducts an exit conference for the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) last November 28, at the Gordon College Main Building, Function Hall.

Vying for one Level III program, five Level II programs, and two candidate programs this year, the institution’s big step is a leap for development, embodying character, excellence, and service along the way.

“This is
label: gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025/]
tep is a leap for development, embodying character, excellence, and service along the way.

“This is not the end,” Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano opened the exit conference of this year’s accreditation with a firm statement.

The accreditors commended the institution’s strengths including the Library Ambassadors Organization (LAO), community extension programs, and professionalism.

Meanwhile, they also recommended improvements such as stronger programs to supplement both students’ and teachers’ needs, more tangible and sustainable community extensions, and subjects that focus on application rather th
label: gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025/]
more tangible and sustainable community extensions, and subjects that focus on application rather than theories.

As Dr. Elizabeth Montero, the supervising accreditor said in her message, “The new oil now is consistency,” a reminder to stay true and stand into the lenses of continuous development where growth is evident and seen.

In response, Ms. Geraldine Raydanas and Mrs. Rowenalyn Sagun expressed their utmost gratitude and promised to improve in the areas mentioned.

Ending the program, Dr. Kristin Joy Mendoza, secretary of GC’s Board of Trustees, left a valuable statement regarding instit
label: gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-a-milestone-in-alcucoa-2025/]
Kristin Joy Mendoza, secretary of GC’s Board of Trustees, left a valuable statement regarding institutionalization and sustainability.

“Every accreditation is an opportunity to innovate and fill the spaces where we can do better,” Dr. Mendoza emphasized.

Meanwhile, the results of the 2025 accreditation are set to be announced in the next few weeks. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Saturday, October 18, 2025**

GORDON COLLEGE MAIN CAMPUS —Gordon College (GC) experiences an increase in its total student population for Academic Year 2025–2026, highlighted by a significant surge in freshmen enrollment across all college departments.

According to the official report from the Office of the College Registrar, the total student population rose from 8,625 this Academic Year (A.Y.) 2025-2026, in contrast to the 7,505 recorded Gcians last 2024–2025.

Furthermore, the numbe
label: gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year/]
r (A.Y.) 2025-2026, in contrast to the 7,505 recorded Gcians last 2024–2025.

Furthermore, the number of Level 1 students also recorded a drastic jump from 2,731 to 3,127 freshmen.

The College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) continues to lead as the largest department, with 2,431 total students and 767 freshmen for this academic year.

The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) recorded a population of 1,642 students and 676 freshmen.

The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) also experienced a notable increase in freshmen enrollees, from 468 last year to 663 this y
label: gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year

content:
[Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year/]
t (CHTM) also experienced a notable increase in freshmen enrollees, from 468 last year to 663 this year.

While the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) saw an increase from 422 to 500 freshmen.

The College of Computer Studies (CCS) also recorded additional enrollees, welcoming 521 freshmen this year compared to the previous 442 record.

In line with its continuous growth, the CBA has officially moved into its new home at the GC Annex Building in Kalaklan, marking a new chapter for the college. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)

**Friday, October 17, 2025**

Due to the rising cases of Influenza-like illnesses, the City Health Office conducts disinfection procedures at 1:00pm, October 17, Gordon College Main and Annex Building.

With this, Face-to-face classes shifted to online mode in the afternoon and will resume the following day, October 18.

Students and campus personnel who are experiencing symptoms of flu are advised to wear a face mask to ensure safety.| Jasmine Zea
